summary: In this very brief early post, DHH shares a piece of advice from Thomas Madsen-Mygdal about the lessons of the dot-com bust. Rather than blaming external forces like markets, journalists, or the economy for failures, the focus should be on personal reflection and learning. The core message is that the real value from the bust era is the knowledge gained, not the grievances accumulated. DHH endorses the idea of introspection over finger-pointing.
key_points: The most valuable takeaway from the dot-com crash is personal knowledge and lessons learned, not blame
External blame (markets, journalists, economy) is less productive than self-reflection
Hindsight should be used for self-improvement rather than recrimination
money_quotes: The most important thing all of us can take from the last years are what we've learned.
Not what the market, journalists, economy, etc. did wrong, but what we would have done differently with the power of the hindsight and knowledge that we've got today.
Stop looking out, start looking in.
key_insight: The real dividend of failure is the knowledge you extract from it through honest self-reflection, not the blame you assign to external forces.
